title: The Cat's Third Eyelid 🐱 Nictitating Membrane Explained
description: Cats have three eyelids, not two. Discover the nictitating membrane and its real purpose. What does it mean when you see that pink eyelid? Real veterinary data on this hidden organ and what its sudden appearance is trying to tell you.

segments.voice: If you've ever seen a strange pinkish or whitish flap appear in the corner of your cat's eye, you weren't imagining it. That's their third eyelid. Most cat owners go years without ever noticing it. When you do see it, it's usually trying to tell you something.

Welcome to CatBrothers. Cats, like many animals, have three eyelids — an upper, a lower, and a third one called the nictitating membrane. The name comes from the Latin nictare, meaning to blink. It's a translucent veil tucked into the inner corner of the eye, ready to deploy when needed.

The third eyelid lives in the inner corner, near the tear duct. Most of the time it sits hidden, sweeping across the eye in microseconds without you ever noticing. It's fast, semi-transparent, and one of the most useful structures in the feline eye.

When you can clearly see the third eyelid, that's usually a clue. The membrane is supposed to be invisible during normal awake behavior. If it's hanging halfway across the eye, the body is signaling something — illness, dehydration, stress, or deep sleep.

Illness is the most common reason. Cats with viral or bacterial infections often show the third eyelid prominently. Upper respiratory infections, dehydration, gastrointestinal issues — all of these can push the membrane forward without the cat even knowing.

The membrane also houses a critical structure called the gland of the third eyelid. This gland produces around 30 to 50% of the tear film that keeps the eye lubricated. If it dries out, the entire eye suffers. Cats can develop chronic dry eye if this gland is damaged.

Its main job is protecting the cornea from physical damage. It also clears debris, similar to a windshield wiper. During rapid head movement or close combat with prey, the third eyelid sweeps across the eye and shields the cornea from accidental injury.

If you notice the third eyelid showing in both eyes for more than a day, see a vet. It can indicate Horner's syndrome, viral infection, or even gastrointestinal parasites. The condition called Haw's syndrome specifically links third eyelid prolapse to digestive problems in cats.

Horner's syndrome is a neurological condition affecting the sympathetic nerves around the eye. Cats with Horner's typically show the third eyelid permanently across the eye, along with a constricted pupil and a drooping upper eyelid. It looks alarming but is often treatable.

Eye irritation or mild infections will also push the third eyelid forward. It's a built-in defense response. The body is essentially shielding the eye while it heals. Treat the underlying issue and the membrane retreats on its own.

Some medications, especially sedatives and anti-anxiety drugs, can cause the third eyelid to become more visible as a side effect. If your cat just had surgery or is on a calming medication, expect the membrane to show up briefly without alarm.

Young cats sometimes develop a temporary condition where both third eyelids stay partially exposed for weeks. It's typically self-resolving and tied to mild gastrointestinal upsets. Worth a vet check, but rarely an emergency.

Then there's cherry eye. The technical name is gland prolapse of the nictitating membrane. The gland physically pops out of position, sticking out of the eye corner like a small red lump. It's more common in dogs, but cats can develop it too. It's not painful at first, but it requires surgery to fix.

Cherry eye is usually treated by tucking the gland back into place surgically. Removal is rarely recommended anymore, since it can cause permanent dry eye. Modern veterinary techniques preserve the gland whenever possible.

What's wild is the chemical makeup of the third eyelid's secretions. The fluid contains immunoglobulins — antibodies that fight bacteria and viruses on contact. Each blink is essentially a microscopic immune sweep across the surface of the eye.

Some cats simply have a more visible third eyelid by nature. It's not always a sign of trouble. Individual variation is real. If your cat has always shown a slight pink corner and remains otherwise healthy, that may just be their normal baseline.

Still, sudden changes deserve attention. If you notice the third eyelid appearing where it never did before, or staying out longer than usual, book a vet visit. Eye issues progress fast in cats. Catching them early can save vision.

So the third eyelid is one of those quiet, brilliant pieces of feline anatomy. It cleans, protects, lubricates, and sometimes whispers warnings about your cat's health. Most owners never see it. When you do, take a closer look — your cat may be telling you something important.
